coredata.dc:title: Chiral carbon dot-modified polymethacrylate resin for adsorption and detection of rare earth ions via tryptophan on-off ratiometric fluorescence
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaoqiong
Bai, Qingyan
Yan, Mingjia
Wang, Haiping
Xu, Yunjia
Ma, Shujuan
Bo, Chunmiao
Ou, Junjie
coredata.dc:description: The chirality of nanostructures plays a crucial role in many applications. Herein, novel chiral carbon dots (L-CD and D-CD) were facilely prepared using L/D-tryptophan (Try) and L-tartaric acid via a one-step hydrothermal microwave-assisted method. The as-synthesized L-CD and D-CD emitted fluorescence with two peaks at 350 and 450 nm under UV irradiation, showing response to various amino acids. By incorporating L-CD into monodisperse polymethacrylate resin, a dual-functional adsorbent (MAR@CD) was fabricated. In the presence of trace amounts of Try, the fluorescence emission of MAR@CD was greatly enhanced, enabling quantitative detection of Pr3+ with on-off fluorescence analysis. On-off ratiometric fluorescence method exhibited the lowest limit of detection (LOD=3σ/S) of 0.69 μmol L−1 for Pr3+, which was lower than those of direct fluorescence spectrometry (4.67 μmol L−1) and on-off fluorescence method (4.06 μmol L−1). In addition, MAR@CD exhibited excellent dispersibility in water and was utilized to adsorb Pr3+. Maximum adsorption capacity of MAR@CD reached 40.5 mg g−1 (actual water environment was 22.4 mg g−1), and adsorption equilibrium was achieved within 120 min, indicating the potential of MAR@CD for rapid adsorption and detection of Pr3+ in environmental scenarios.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral
Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon dots
Rare earth ions
Adsorption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of amino acids via ratiometric fluorescence method
Detection of Pr3+ via L-Try on-off fluorescence
Design and preparation of chiral CDs and MAR@CD
Fluorescence response of CDs to various amino acids
Quantitative detection of Try via ratiometric fluorescence method
Fluorescence selectivity of L-CD to L-Try
Quantitative detection of Pr3+ via Try-induced on-off ratiometric fluorescence method

Ou.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic presentation of the synthesis and application of chiral CDs and MAR@CD.
TEM images of (a, b) L-CD, and SEM images of (c) MAR and (d) MAR@CD, and TEM images of (e,f) MAR@CD.
(a) FT-IR spectra of L-CD, D-CD, MAR and MAR@CD, (b) circular dichroism spectra of L-CD and D-CD, (c) XPS spectra and (d) nitrogen sorption of MAR and MAR@CD.
Fluorescence emission spectra of L-CD at different (a) excitation wavelength and (b) pH, fluorescence intensity at the emission wavelength of (c) 350 and (d) 450 nm in different solutions.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of L-CD at different L-Try concentrations, relationships between (b) fluorescence intensity of L-CD at 350 nm and L-Try concentration by direct fluorescence spectrometry and (c) fluorescence intensity ratio of L-CD at 350 nm to 450 nm and L-Try concentration by ratiometric fluorescence method. (d) Fluorescence intensity of L-CD at the emission wavelength of 350 and 450 nm in binary and multicomponent mixtures.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of MAR@CD under different excitation wavelength, fluorescence emission spectra of MAR@CD in the presence of different Pr3+ solutions (b) without and (c) with L-Try, relationships between fluorescence intensity of MAR@CD at 350 nm and Pr3+ concentration by (d) direct fluorescence spectrometry (e) on-off fluorescence method and (f) fluorescence intensity ratio of MAR@CD at 350 nm to 450 nm and Pr3+concentration by on-off ratiometric fluorescence method.
Fluorescence lifetime of MAR@CD at emission wavelength of (a, c) 350 nm and (b, d) 450 nm in (a, b) pure water and (c, d) Pr3+ solution.
(a) Adsorption isothermal curves and (b) adsorption kinetic curves of MAR@CD.
Fluorescence intensity of L-CD in binary and multicomponent mixtures.
Fluorescence intensity of MAR@CD in different solutions.
Fluorescence lifetime of MAR@CD in pure water and Pr3+ solution.
Comparison of MAR@CD with other materials for detection of rare earth ion.

head.ce:title: Chiral carbon dot-modified polymethacrylate resin for adsorption and detection of rare earth ions via tryptophan on-off ratiometric fluorescence

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The chirality of nanostructures plays a crucial role in many applications. Herein, novel chiral carbon dots (L-CD and D-CD) were facilely prepared using L/D-tryptophan (Try) and L-tartaric acid via a one-step hydrothermal microwave-assisted method. The as-synthesized L-CD and D-CD emitted fluorescence with two peaks at 350 and 450 nm under UV irradiation, showing response to various amino acids. By incorporating L-CD into monodisperse polymethacrylate resin, a dual-functional adsorbent (MAR@CD) was fabricated. In the presence of trace amounts of Try, the fluorescence emission of MAR@CD was greatly enhanced, enabling quantitative detection of Pr3+ with on-off fluorescence analysis. On-off ratiometric fluorescence method exhibited the lowest limit of detection (LOD=3σ/S) of 0.69 μmol L−1 for Pr3+, which was lower than those of direct fluorescence spectrometry (4.67 μmol L−1) and on-off fluorescence method (4.06 μmol L−1). In addition, MAR@CD exhibited excellent dispersibility in water and was utilized to adsorb Pr3+. Maximum adsorption capacity of MAR@CD reached 40.5 mg g−1 (actual water environment was 22.4 mg g−1), and adsorption equilibrium was achieved within 120 min, indicating the potential of MAR@CD for rapid adsorption and detection of Pr3+ in environmental scenarios.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral nanostructures have garnered significant attention in various fields due to their unique optical and electronic properties[1–4]. The chirality of these nanostructures arises from their asymmetric molecular arrangement, resulting in distinct interactions with polarized light[5,6]. As a result, chirality plays a crucial role in applications such as optical sensing, catalysis, drug delivery and chiral separation[7,8]. In recent years, the innovation of sensor technology has significantly promoted the development of various fields, among which Raman sensors, photoelectric chemical sensors and fluorescence sensors are particularly eye-catching, and fluorescence sensors perform well in the detection of low concentrations of substances[9–15]. Carbon-based chiral nanostructures, such as carbon dots (CDs), have emerged as promising candidates for various applications due to their exceptional optical property, biocompatibility and easy functionalization[16–19]. CDs are zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles with size-tunable fluorescence emission and excellent photostability[20,21]. They have attracted significant attention for their potential in bioimaging, biosensing and fluorescence detection[22,23]. There were many reports on utilizing the fluorescence properties of CD to achieve excellent performance in fluorescence detection[24,25].

Nowadays, various methods such as electrochemical oxidation, combustion/thermal methods, chemical transformations, microwave heating, arc discharge and laser ablation can be employed for the synthesis of CDs[26–28]. Among these methods, bottom-up microwave-assisted synthesis has gained rapid development in recent years due to its green, environmentally friendly and sustainable characteristics[29–32]. With the development of chiral CDs, it has shown broad application prospects in many fields[33]. Due to its excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and biodegradability, chiral CDs can be utilized for biomedical applications such as cell imaging, drug delivery, and enzyme-like catalytic regulation[34,35]. In addition, chirality endowed stronger fluorescence properties and stability, and chiral CDs with excellent fluorescence characteristics can not only detect metal ions, but also serve as chemical or biological sensors to provide accurate analysis and detection[36,37]. During the synthesis of chiral CDs via cysteine and citric acid, Victoria and coworkers observed that the chirality signal intensity of CDs decreased as the synthetic temperature increased from 160 °C to 220 °C[38]. Branz et al. synthesized chiral CDs based on cysteine through copper-catalyzed reactions and further investigated the chiral evolution during the reaction process. They observed that prolonging reaction time could also lead to a decrease in the chiral signal[39]. Coincidentally, Li et al. successfully obtained chiral CDs by using cysteine as a sole reactant through hydrothermal treatment at 60 °C, avoiding the destruction of chiral environment at high temperature and achieving a high photoluminescence quantum yield of 41.26 %[40]. Therefore, it is advisable to avoid high-temperature environment and shorten reaction time as much as possible during the preparation of chiral CDs, as these conditions may potentially damage the chiral structure of CDs. As a result, there is an urgent need to design and fabricate novel chiral CDs.

Herein, a kind of novel chiral CDs (L-CD and D-CD) was fabricated through a simple hydrothermal microwave assisted method using L/D-tryptophan (Try) and L-tartaric acid as precursors. Subsequently, the fluorescence response of CD to various amino acids was measured. Then, CDs were modified onto monodisperse poly(glycidyl methacrylate-co-polyethylene glycol dimethacrylate) (named poly(GMA-co-EDMA)) resin to prepare a dual-functional material (noted MAR@CD). In addition, an on-off ratiometric fluorescence method was established to target the substance in the presence of Try, and the effectiveness of different methods for detecting rare earth ions by MAR@CD was compared. The adsorption performance of MAR@CD for Pr3+ was evaluated through isotherm and kinetic adsorption experiments.

Chiral CD was prepared via one-step hydrothermal microwave-assisted method. Typically, 615 mg of L-Try and 450.3 mg of L-tartaric acid were placed in 15 mL of deionized water. Then, 0.3 mL of phosphoric acid was added dropwise to the solution under ultrasound. The transparent mixture was subjected to microwave assistance at 100 ℃ and 500 W for 5 min and subsequently diluted with 15 mL of water. The obtained solution was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter. The filtrate was then placed in a dialysis bag (1000 Da) and dialyzed in water for 48 h. After another round of filtration and rotary evaporation, the resulting product was named L-CD.
By replacing equal amount of L-Try with D-Try, D-CD was also prepared with the above-mentioned procedures.

According to our previous report [41], monodisperse poly(GMA-co-EDMA) microspheres (noted MAR) were prepared by one-step seeded swelling polymerization. Then, 1.5 g of MAR were dispersed in 100 mL of 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH solution, and then 2 mL of L-CD were added. The mixture was thoroughly mixed by ultrasound, and the reaction was carried out under shaking at 60 °C for 10 h. The product was washed with water and ethanol until neutral, followed by vacuum drying to obtain MAR@CD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of amino acids via ratiometric fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: Fourteen compounds (L/D-Try, L/D-lysine, L/D-tartaric acid, L/D-alanine, L/D-proline, L/D-aspartic acid and L/D-cysteine) were selected to evaluate the fluorescence response of L-CD and D-CD in solutions under the same concentration (400 μmol L−1). The fluorescence intensity of the mixture was recorded at 350 nm and 450 nm using a fluorescence spectrophotometer with single-wavelength excitation at the maximum excitation wavelength. The measurements were performed 3 times under the same conditions and error bars were plotted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Pr3+ via L-Try on-off fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to Section 2.3 , the MAR@CD suspension was mixed with different concentrations (0–400 μmol L−1) of rare earth solution and allowed to stand for 5 min. The mixture was then excited at a single wavelength of 270 nm, and the fluorescence intensity of the mixture at 350 nm and 450 nm was recorded.

Quantitative analysis was conducted to investigate the relationship between Pr3+ concentration and the fluorescence intensity of MAR@CD. The concentration of Pr3+ was measured in the range of 0–400 μmol L−1. A series of mixed solutions containing MAR@CD and different concentrations of Pr3+ were prepared. The concentration of MAR@CD was kept constant to maintain a suitable fluorescence intensity for detection. Fluorescence intensity was measured 3 times and error bars were plotted.

Quantum yield (Ф F) was measured using quinine sulfate as the reference [42]. The quantum yield of CD@MIP was calculated using the following:
(1)
Ф F=Ф R ∫I A R n 2/∫I R A n R 2

where the subscripts F and R refer to the fluorophore and reference compound, respectively. Ф is the fluorescence quantum yield, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (less than 0.05), ∫I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, and n is the refractive index of the solvents.

The fluorescence lifetime of MAR@CD in pure water and in Pr3+ solution (100 μmol L−1) was determined. Under an excitation wavelength of 295 nm, fluorescence decay curves were plotted. Fitting was carried out using single exponential and double exponential functions, as follows:
(2)
I (t) =B1exp(-t/τ
1 )
(3)
I (t) =B1exp(-t/τ
1 )+B2exp(-t/τ
2 )

where t (ns) is the decay time, I (t) is the emission intensity, τ (ns) represents the fluorescence decay time, and B is the stretch parameter. When the fitting parameter (X2) is close to 1, it indicates that the fitted fluorescence lifetime is in good agreement with the actual fluorescence lifetime decay.

The adsorption of Pr3+ onto MAR@CD was investigated through isothermal and kinetic adsorption experiments, and the isotherms and kinetic parameters were determined. The adsorption experiments were performed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

In the isothermal adsorption experiment, 15 mg of CD@MIP were dispersed in 100 mL of Pr3+ solution with different concentrations (0–60 mg L−1). The mixture was then stirred for 8 h. After centrifugation and filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane, the adsorbed Pr3+ solution was obtained. The amount of adsorption was calculated according to the following:
(4)
Q e =(C 0 -C e)V/m

where Q e (mg g−1) is equilibrium adsorption capacity, C 0 and C e (mg L−1) are the initial concentration and equilibrium concentration, respectively, V (mL) is the volume of aqueous solution, and m (mg) is the mass of materials. The isothermal adsorption data are further analyzed by Langmuir and Freundlich isothermal models, whose formulas are as follows:
(5)
C e/Q e =C e/Q max +1/K L Q max
(6) lgQ e=lgC e/n+lgK F

where Q max (mg g−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity, C e (mg mL−1) is the concentration in equilibrium state, 1/n is the empirical coefficient of Freundlich, K L (mL mg−1) and K F (mg g−1) are Langmuir and Freundlich constant, respectively.

In the kinetic adsorption, MAR@CD (15 mg) was dispersed in 100 mL of Pr3+ solution with a concentration of 15 mg L−1. The suspension was stirred at a constant temperature of 200 rpm for different time periods (0–240 min). After centrifugation, the adsorbed solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm. The dynamic adsorption data were further analyzed by pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models, whose formulas are as follows:
(7) ln(Q e-Q t)=lnQ e-K 1t
(8)
t/Q t=t/Q e+1/K 2 Q e 2
where K 1 and K 2 are the reaction rate constants of pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic model, respectively, and Q t (mg g−1) is the adsorption capacity at a particular time.

The adsorption capability of MAR@CD for Pr3+ in actual samples was measured. Lake water (Ming Lake, Yinchuan, China) was chosen as the sample. After centrifugal, filtration and the addition of Pr3+, a wastewater sample containing Pr3+ (50 mg L−1) was obtained. Then 15 mg of MAR@CD were mixed with 100 mL of this wastewater sample. After shaking at room temperature for 2 h, the supernatant was collected to measure the concentration of Pr3+ and calculate the adsorption amount.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and preparation of chiral CDs and MAR@CD
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of chiral and dual-emitting L-CD and D-CD is based on bottom-up microwave-assisted hydrothermal method utilizing low-cost and eco-friendly precursors L-Try and tartaric acid, as shown in Fig. 1. Compared with conventional hydrothermal method (From hours to minutes), the microwave method can significantly shorten the reaction time, although the fluorescence yield will decrease. The ratiometric fluorescence method helps to eliminate instrument and other interferences[43–46]. Therefore, a Try-induced on-off ratiometric fluorescence method to target the substance was established and compared with direct fluorescence spectrometry method. MAR, a kind of commercial material, was selected to integrate with CDs through “click” reaction of epoxy and amine, and the resulting MAR@CD composite exhibited excellent dispersibility in water and applied. MAR@CD was easily separated from the environment through filtration after adsorption and could be reused multiple times after eluting the adsorbed substances, reducing material wastage and fluorescence pollution. Overall, the entire preparation process and application would realize the vision of green chemistry.

The morphology of L-CD, MAR, and MAR@CD was evaluated through TEM and SEM, as illustrated in Fig. 2. The synthesized CDs were observed to be spherical and excellently dispersed with approximate 4–5 nm in diameter, and distinct lattice fringes were visible (Fig. 2a and b). Through a seeded swelling polymerization, MAR with an average particle size of about 5 μm was successfully prepared (Fig. 2c). The carbon dot-modified MAR@CD maintained spherical morphology and same size of the pristine MAR (Fig. 2d), indicating that the incorporation of CDs did not alter the surface morphology of MAR. The TEM images of MAR@CD are shown in Fig. 2e-f. It can be seen that there are layers on the edges of MAR@CD and a layer of carbon dots at the periphery with approximate a thickness of 54–73 nm.

The types and compositions of functional groups on the surface of materials were determined with FTIR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra of L-CD, D-CD, MAR and MAR@CD are displayed in Fig. 3a. It was evident that the vibrational peaks at 3130 and 3440 cm−1 were attributed to symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration modes of -NH2 group in CDs. The peak at 1630 cm−1 was related to C-N stretching vibration. In summary, these observations suggested that CDs contained a large number of amino groups. The spectra of L-CD and D-CD are almost identical, which is due to their same structure being a pair of enantiomers. In the spectrum of MAR@CD, a broad peak at 3600–3200 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibrations of -NH2 and -OH groups. The absorption peak of the -CO group was located at 1730 cm−1. Dominant spectrum of MAR also emerged in the spectrum of MAR@CD, as the immobilized CDs took up only a small portion.

The optical chirality of L-CD and D-CD, as measured by circular dichroism spectroscopy, exhibits distinct spectral profiles for each enantiomer due to differential absorption of left-handed and right-handed circularly polarized light. In a chiral enantiomeric pair, one enantiomer demonstrates positive optical rotation at a specific wavelength due to the positive Cotton effect, while the other displays an equal but opposite optical rotation at the same wavelength (negative Cotton effect). As shown in Fig. 3b, the presence of sharp chiroptical peak at 220 nm suggested both L-CD and D-CD possessed a large number of conjugated structures and relatively stable chiral environment within the molecule where the corresponding electronic transitions were restricted by the rigid internal structure. Additionally, the occurrence of multiple smaller corresponding peaks throughout the spectra of L-CD and D-CD indicated that the synthesized CD inherited a few chiral properties of the precursors.

Surface chemical composition of the materials was characterized by XPS. The complete XPS spectra of MAR and MAR@CD are presented in Fig. 3c. The main elements of MAR consist of carbon and oxygen, with carbon content of 65.1 % and oxygen content of 33.3 %, while no nitrogen was detected. Upon the addition of CD, the carbon content of MAR@CD decreased to 63.9 %, oxygen content increased to 35.6 %, and nitrogen content was measured at 1.34 %. These results demonstrated that the MAR@CD was still monodisperse polymeric resin and the CD on the surface only accounted for a small part. The presence of a small amount of nitrogen in MAR@CD indicated successful incorporation of CD with MAR.

The BET surface area, pore size and pore volume of MAR@CD and MAR were ascertained through BET analysis, as depicted in Table S1. The specific surface area of MAR@CD was 35.9±0.5 m² g−1, which was slightly lower than that of MAR (44.3±0.4 m² g−1). Additionally, the pore volume of MAR@CD was measured at 0.14 cm³ g−1, and smaller than that of MAR (0.18 cm³ g−1). These data suggested that the introduction of CD led to a slight reduction of surface area. The nitrogen adsorption isotherms of MAR@CD and MAR are depicted in Fig. 3d, showing a rapid rise in nitrogen adsorption volume as the relative pressure exceeded 0.85 and the existence of macropores in them. The pore size distribution is depicted in Fig. S1, demonstrating the existence of macropores and mesopores in around 24 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs to various amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of diluted CD solutions under different excitation wavelengths were plotted, as shown in Fig. 4 and S2. Two peaks at about 350 nm and 450 nm emerged in the fluorescence spectrum of L-CD (Fig. 4a). When the excitation wavelength was 250 nm, the maximum emission wavelength was 450 nm. However, the maximum emission wavelength was 350 nm when the excitation wavelength was 270 nm. The quantum yield of L-CD after dialysis was calculated to be 8.35 %. To further determine the nature of these two fluorescence peaks, fluorescence spectra of CDs were plotted at pH 3 and 11 with an excitation wavelength of 250 nm, as depicted in Fig. 4b. Under acidic condition, fluorescence quenching occurred at fluorescence emission wavelength of 350 nm, while fluorescence enhancement was observed at fluorescence emission wavelength of 450 nm. Obviously, the opposite phenomenon was observed under basic condition.

The fluorescence intensities of L-CD in various amino acid and tartaric acid solutions were measured at 350 nm and 450 nm, and the results are shown in Fig. 4c and d. L-CD exhibited fluorescence response to a variety of amino acids. The fluorescence enhancement at the emission wavelength of 350 nm occurred in the presence of Try and lysine, but fluorescence quenching emerged in the presence of tartaric acid and cysteine. At the excitation wavelength of 450 nm, fluorescence quenching occurred in the presence of lysine, reaching approximately 51 %, while fluorescence enhancement was observed with tartaric acid and cysteine (26 % and 17 %, respectively). It should be pointed that the fluorescence intensity of L-CD also depended on enantiomers of these amino acids. For example, the fluorescence intensity of L-CD at 350 nm in L-Try solution significantly increased from 11,273 to 105,767, which was slightly higher than that in D-Try solution (96,920), and the fluorescence intensity in L-lysine solution (15,546) was greater than that in D-lysine solution (14,491). These results demonstrated that L-CD possessed enantio-responses to different configurations of chiral compounds. Incidentally, similar phemomena were also observed in D-CD (Fig. S2c and d). Thus, it was possible to distinguish enantiomers of Try, lysine, cysteine and so on.

The effect of pH in various amino acids solution on fluorescence intensity of L-CD was investigated, as shown in Fig. S3. The result demonstrated that L-CD lost its fluorescence response to most of amino acids under strong acidic (pH=3) or alkaline (pH=11) condition. However, in the presence of L-Try or D-Try, L-CD still exhibited obvious fluorescence enhancement at 350 nm. Under the condition of pH=3, the fluorescence intensity of L-CD in L-Try remarkably increased from 4196 to 58,427, and under the condition of pH=11, it further increased to 204,013. The phenomenon of increased fluorescence intensity at 350 nm was suppressed under acidic condition, while it was enhanced under alkaline condition. The similar phenomenon was also observed in D-CD (Fig. S4), indicating that the response of CD to chiral substances of different configurations would be affected by environmental factors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of Try via ratiometric fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of L-CD in L-Try and D-Try solutions at different concentrations were measured, as shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S5. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of L-CD at 350 nm increased significantly with the concentration of Try, while the fluorescence intensity at 450 nm did not change significantly compared with 350 nm. The fluorescence detection of L-Try and D-Try by L-CD was conducted by both direct fluorescence spectrometry and ratiometric fluorescence method. The L-CD was placed in L-Try solutions with different concentrations, and the fluorescence emission spectra are presented in Fig. 5a. The direct fluorescence spectrometry involves plotting and linearly fitting a relationship between the fluorescence intensity at 350 nm (I350) and the concentration of Try. As shown in Fig. 5b, the change of fluorescence intensity included two processes. (i) At relatively lower concentrations of Try (≤100 μmol L−1), the fluorescence intensity of L-CD increased rapidly with an increase of concentration due to sufficient space on the CD surface for interaction with Try molecules. (ii) As the concentration of Try further increased (>100 μmol L−1), most of the fluorescent active groups on the CD surface already interacted sufficiently with Try molecules, so the rate of increase in fluorescence intensity with concentration was lower (the slope of the fitted line decreased). When the concentration of Try exceeded 500 μmol L−1, the linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Try concentration gradually deteriorated (R<0.99).

The ratiometric fluorescence method involved plotting and linearly fitting the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 350 nm and 450 nm (I350/I450) against the concentration of Try. As shown in Fig. 5c, two stages also appeared in ratiometric fluorescence method. Using the 3σ rule (σ=S0/S, where S0 is the standard deviation after 11 blank measurements and S is the slope of the fitted curve), the limit LOD for direct fluorescence spectrometry was calculated to be 0.92 μmol L−1, while the LOD for ratiometric fluorescence method was 0.34 μmol L−1. As a result, quantitative detection of Try was sucessively carried out via two methods, but the ratiometric fluorescence method allowed more sensitive measurement of lower concentrations of Try.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence selectivity of L-CD to L-Try
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence selectivity was investigated through binary and multicomponent mixtures, selecting L-lysine, L-cysteine and L-aspartic acid as interfering substances. These substances might coexist with the target substance (L-Try) in real environments and demonstrated to potentially affect the fluorescence intensity of L-CD. The concentration of L-Try was 50 μmol L−1, and the concentration of interfering amino acids was 100 μmol L−1.△I350 is the change of fluorescence intensity at 350 nm compared with the L-Try group; △I450, the change at 450 nm; and △I350/I450, the change in the ratio of fluorescence intensities at 350 nm and 450 nm. As shown in Fig. 5d and Table 1, when Try coexisted with L-cysteine and L-aspartic acid in binary and multicomponent mixtures, the error in Try detection by direct fluorescence spectrometry and ratio fluorescence method was not significant (≤3 %). When there was only L-lysine and L-Try in the solution, a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of L-CD at 450 nm (43.1 %) was observed. This indicated that the presence of L-lysine in simple environments interfered with the detection of Try, making the ratio fluorescence method unsuitable under these conditions. Try detection should be conducted by measuring the fluorescence intensity at 350 nm. In summary, L-CD enabled the detection of Try amidst high concentrations of other amino acid interferences, demonstrating practical application value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of Pr3+ via Try-induced on-off ratiometric fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: By modifying CD to MAR, the obtained MAR@CD also exhibited fluorescence. The fluorescence curves of MAR@CD under different excitation wavelengths are shown in Fig. 6a, and the excitation wavelength of 270 nm was finally selected in subsequent experiments. We also measured the fluorescence changes of MAR@CD in the presence of 5 rare earth ions. The detailed result was given in Table 2. Without adding Try, MAR@CD exhibited fluorescence quenching for these rare earth ions. Following the application of ratiometric fluorescence method (＞10 %), no significant responses were observed except for Gd3+ (14.83 %, △I350/I450). Hence, without the fluorescence enhancement of Try, it is difficult to selectively and sensitively detect rare earth ions via fluorescence spectrometry and ratiometric fluorescence method. However, upon adding a few L-Try, a noticeable fluorescence response to Pr3+ was observed. When the L-Try-induced on-off ratiometric fluorescence method was adopted, the change rate (△I350/I450) for Pr3+ reached 18.17 %, which clearly distinguished from other rare earth ions (＜10 %). It is mainly due to the fact that MAR@CD exhibited more significant fluorescence quenching at 450 nm in the presence of Pr3+.

According to the above experimental results, Pr3+ was quantitatively analyzed with MAR@CD by direct fluorescence spectrometry, on-off fluorescence method and on-off ratiometric fluorescence method. Comparing with fluorescence emission spectra of MAR@CD (Fig. 6a), remarkably fluorescence quenching occurred in the presence of different Pr3+ solutions without adding L-Try (Fig. 6b). The relationship between fluorescence intensity of MAR@CD at 350 nm and Pr3+ concentration via direct fluorescence spectrometry was plotted in Fig. 6d. The fluorescence spectra of MAR@CD at different Pr3+ concentrations were also measured in the presence of trace L-Try to establish on-off fluorescence method, as presented in Fig. 6c, and the standard curve was plotted in Fig. 6e. In addition, a relationship between the ratio of fluorescence intensity at 350 nm to 450 nm and Pr3+concentration was plotted in Fig. 6f to reflect L-Try-induced on-off ratiometric fluorescence method. Three methods for Pr3+ detection exhibited a two-phase pattern and robust linear regression coefficients. Notably, the highest coefficient (R=0.998) was achieved by the on-off fluorescence method. The LOD of L-Try-induced on-off ratiometric fluorescence method was 0.69 μmol L−1, which was lower than those of direct fluorescence spectrometry and on-off fluorescence method (4.67 and 4.06 μmol L−1, respectively). Additionally, the on-off ratiometric fluorescence method demonstrated excellent accuracy, as evidenced by the calculated smaller relative standard deviation when the concentration is less than 40 μmol L−1 (＜0.16). Therefore, the on-off ratiometric fluorescence method was the most suitable for quantitative analysis of Pr3+ via MAR@CD.

The fluorescence lifetime of MAR@CD was measured, as shown in Fig. 7 and Table 3. In pure water, the fluorescence lifetime of MAR@CD at an emission wavelength of 350 nm (λem=350 nm) was 4.38 ns, and the fluorescence lifetime was 16.98 ns at an emission wavelength of 450 nm. When MAR@CD was in a Pr3+ solution, the fluorescence lifetime at an emission wavelength of 350 nm increased to 4.61 ns, while the fluorescence lifetime at an emission wavelength of 450 nm decreased to 16.53 ns. This phenomenon can be explained from two aspects. First, when the energy level of rare earth ions is close to that of L-CDs, non-radiative transitions (quenching without emitting light) occur, transferring energy to the rare earth ions and leading to fluorescence quenching[47]. In our case, the indole group of MAR@CD and Try can coordinate with the rare earth ions, causing 350 nm fluorescence quenching but increasing fluorescence lifetime. At 450 nm, the complex led to a faster energy dissipation path, thereby reducing the fluorescence lifetime. The second mechanism is energy transfer: The increase in fluorescence lifetime at 350 nm was due to energy transfer between Pr3+ and the excited state associated with that fluorescence peak. The Pr3+, acting as energy acceptors, received energy from the fluorescent material (energy donor). This transfer extended the lifetime of the excited state because the energy was not immediately released during the transfer to the Pr3+, thereby resulting in an increased fluorescence lifetime.

Herein, although the maximum adsorption capacity of PAD@COF was marginally lower than that of hydrazone-linked COF, it exhibited superior adsorption performance compared to other 6 kinds of copper ion adsorption materials. Furthermore, PAD@COF demonstrated a shorter equilibrium adsorption time in comparison to magnetic hydrogel, activated carbon and imine-based COF materials.

A few researches on the detection of rare earth ions are listed in Table 4. Chen et al. observed that Sc3+ can inhibit the acid quenching of complex fluorescent calcein to selectively detect Sc3+ and realize portable on-site analysis of Sc3+ by combining with 3D printing and smart phone programs[48]. Yang and his colleagues synthesized a novel fluorescent probe dtpa-bis(cytosine) for accurate detection of Eu3+ by designing diethylenetriamine pentaacetic acid dianhydride and cytosine amolysis reactions[49]. A hybrid fluorescent chemical sensor prepared by encapsulating organic Schiff base materials on the inner surface of epoxy propoxy-functionalized mesoporous silica was applied in the detection of Sn2+ [50]. Newly developed conjugated adsorbent by Kubra's team was utilized for the detection and recovery of Ce3+ [51]. Recent research has produced paper-based sensors for the detection of Ce3+, and the use of 3D printing and iot integration to generate prototypes, which help to quickly and accurately measure and detect cerium ions[52]. Herein, compared with other detection materials, the limit of detection (LOD) was lower than those of the new fluorescence probe dtpa-bis(cytosine) and paper sensor, but the synthesis of MAR@CD was more environmentally friendly than other methods, and the combination of intelligent devices for rapid detection of rare earth ions is also worth learning.

The adsorption performance of MAR@CD for Pr3+ was evaluated by plotting the adsorption isotherm, as shown in Fig. 8a. Analysis of the adsorption isotherm revealed a rapid increase in the adsorption capacity of MAR@CD for Pr3+ as the concentration of Pr3+ increased. With an increase of the concentration of Pr3+ (>30 mg L−1), the rate of increase in adsorption capacity gradually slowed down, ultimately reaching adsorption equilibrium at a concentration of approximately 40 mg L−1, with a maximum adsorption capacity of 40.5 mg g−1. A further exploration of adsorption mode was conducted through fitting the equilibrium adsorption isotherm, as shown in Fig. S6a and b. The results indicated that the Langmuir model fitting coefficient (RL=0.978) was slightly higher than that of the Freundlich model (RF=0.968), suggesting that monolayer adsorption predominated.

Kinetic adsorption experiment revealed a rapid increase in the adsorption of MAR@CD during the initial stage (within 120 min), following by a gradual decrease in the adsorption rate and reaching adsorption equilibrium at approximate 120 min (Fig. 8b). Fitting with pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models was carried out. As shown in Fig. S6c and d, the correlation coefficient of the pseudo-second-order model for MAR@CD (R2=0.989) was slightly higher than that of the pseudo-first-order model (R1=0.986). The better agreement of the experimental data with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model indicated that chemical adsorption primarily occurred on the surface.

The adsorption ability of MAR@CD for wastewater in real environments is assessed. The adsorption amount of MAR@CD for Pr3+ was 22.4 mg g−1 within 2 h. And the LOD did not change significantly in the lake water sample. This indicated that MAR@CD possessed dual functionality, enabling rapid adsorption and fluorescence detection of Pr3+.

A pair of chiral carbon dots (L-CD and D-CD) has been synthesized by microwave-assisted method using L/D-Try and L-tartaric acid as precursors. Under UV irradiation, the excitation spectra of L-CD and D-CD exhibited two distinct peaks at 350 nm and 450 nm, respectively. Chiral CD was incorporated onto a monodisperse polymethacrylate resin to create a novel MAR@CD material, demonstrating the following advantages. First, compared with either L-CD or D-CD, MAR@CD enhanced the detection selectivity of Pr3+ via L-Try-induced on-off ratiometric fluorescence method. Second, MAR@CD possessed dual functions of simultaneous adsorption and detection of Pr3+. Finally, the microwave-assisted synthesis of CD is environmentally friendly and rapid, but the main limiting factor is the relatively low fluorescence yield. MAR@CD could be easily separated from the solution via filtration, avoiding material waste and fluorescence pollution and thus meeting the principles of green chemistry. It is anticipated that the one-step microwave hydrothermal method for preparing chiral CDs with ratiometric fluorescence property, and the combination of CDs and adsorbent materials to prepare dual-functional materials with fluorescence detection and adsorption capabilities would have broad application prospects.

Xiaoqiong Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Qingyan Bai: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mingjia Yan: Formal analysis, Methodology. Haiping Wang: Investigation. Yunjia Xu: Formal analysis. Shujuan Ma: Supervision. Chunmiao Bo: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. Junjie Ou: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yang, P. Ren, G. Liu, Y. Song, N. Bu, J. Wang, A novel fluorescent probe (dtpa-bis(cytosine)) for detection of Eu(III) in rare earth metal ions, Spectrochim. Acta. A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc. 193 (2018) 357-364.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of rare earth metal Sn(II) ion by organic fluorine Schiff base functionalized periodic mesoporous material in aqueous solution
J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Wang, M. Lv, Z. Wang, M. Zhou, C. Gu, C. Guo, Highly sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of rare earth metal Sn(II) ion by organic fluorine Schiff base functionalized periodic mesoporous material in aqueous solution, J. Photochem. Photobiol. A Chem. 309 (2015) 37-46.

J. Mol. Liq.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Advanced nutrient monitoring: cost-effective cerium ion detection with smart device integration
Biochem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R.R. Lekshmy, M. Maurya, M. Geetha, S. Al-Maadeed, R. Maalej, M.Z. Chaari, R.D. Pyarasani, J. Amalraj, K.K. Sadasivuni, Advanced nutrient monitoring: cost-effective cerium ion detection with smart device integration, Biochem. Eng. J. 209 (2024) 109409.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaoqiong Wang was born in Shaanxi, China. Graduated from Xian Petroleum University in 2022. Currently a graduate student at North Minzu University. Her current research interests include carbon dot, activated carbon and covalent organic frameworks.

Qingyan Bai was born in Beijing, China. Graduated from Beijing Technology and Business University in 2020. He is now a graduate student of North Minzu University. His current research interests include carbon dot, molecularly imprinted polymers, adsorption materials and so on.

Mingjia Yan was born in Shanxi, China. Graduated from Mudanjiang Normal University in 2022. Currently a graduate student at North Minzu University. His current research interests include carbon dots, activated carbon and Janus particle.

Haiping Wang was born in Jilin, China. Graduated from Hexi University in 2018. Currently a graduate student at North Minzu University. His current research interests include molecularly imprinted materials, activated carbon.

Yunjia Xu was born in Shijiazhuang, China. Graduated from Hebei Agricultural University in 2021. Currently a graduate student at North Minzu University. His current research interests include molecularly imprinted materials and carbon dots.

Shujuan Ma was born in Shanxi, China. He is an associate professor at Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics. She received his PhD in 2019. She is mainly engaged in research on chromatographic separation theory, biological sample separation and analysis technology.

Chunmiao Bo was born in Ningxia, China. He is an associate professor at North Minzu University. She received his PhD in 2017. She is mainly engaged in research on preparation of separation and enrichment materials, drug-protein interactions, separation and analysis of complex biological samples.

Junjie Ou was born in Shannxi, China. He was a professor at Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, and currently moved to Northwest University. He received his PhD in 2010. His research focuses on bioseparation and analysis and novel materials.
